runs.interpretation: The elasticity representing the percentage change in labor supply (hours worked) of secondary earners in response to a percentage change in their net-of-tax wage rate, commonly applied in discrete choice labor supply models within U.S. tax microsimulations.
citations: Blundell and MaCurdy (1999) Handbook of Labor Economics
Eissa and Liebman (1996) Labor supply response to the Earned Income Tax Credit
Heckman (1993) What has been learned about labor supply in the past twenty years?
runs.reasoningSummary: Empirical evidence from the U.S. consistently shows that primary earners have low labor supply elasticities, whereas secondary earners—typically married women—exhibit substantially higher responses. Estimates for secondary earners in structural models typically range from 0.2 to 0.8, with microsimulation models often favoring values in the 0.3 to 0.6 range to balance internal consistency with broader labor market evidence.

  "interpretation": "The elasticity representing the percentage change in labor supply (hours worked) of secondary earners in response to a percentage change in their net-of-tax wage rate, commonly applied in discrete choice labor supply models within U.S. tax microsimulations.",

  "quantiles": {

    "Heckman (1993) What has been learned about labor supply in the past twenty years?"
  ],
  "reasoning_summary": "Empirical evidence from the U.S. consistently shows that primary earners have low labor supply elasticities, whereas secondary earners—typically married women—exhibit substantially higher responses. Estimates for secondary earners in structural models typically range from 0.2 to 0.8, with microsimulation models often favoring values in the 0.3 to 0.6 range to balance internal consistency with broader labor market evidence."
}

runs.interpretation: The elasticity of labor supply for secondary earners in the U.S. with respect to the net-of-tax wage rate, specifically the substitution component used in microsimulation models.
citations: Heckman, J. J. (1993). What Has Been Learned About Labor Supply in the Past Twenty Years?
Blundell, R., & MaCurdy, T. (1999). Labor Supply: A Review of Alternative Approaches.
Eissa, N., & Hoynes, H. W. (2004). Taxes and the Labor Market Participation of Married Couples.
runs.reasoningSummary: While primary earners (mostly men) exhibit very low labor supply elasticities, secondary earners (typically married women) show significantly higher responsiveness. Empirical literature often places the compensated (substitution) elasticity for this group between 0.2 and 0.6, with substantial variance depending on whether the margin is participation (extensive) or hours worked (intensive). The estimate accounts for the reality that many microsimulation models calibrate toward the higher end to capture observed policy responses, while pure intensive-margin studies often yield more conservative figures.
runs.rawResponse:
{
  "interpretation": "The elasticity of labor supply for secondary earners in the U.S. with respect to the net-of-tax wage rate, specifically the substitution component used in microsimulation models.",
  "point_estimate": 0.35,
  "quantiles": {
    "p05": 0.05,
    "p25": 0.2,
    "p50": 0.35,
    "p75": 0.55,
    "p95": 0.9
  },
  "citations": [
    "Heckman, J. J. (1993). What Has Been Learned About Labor Supply in the Past Twenty Years?",
    "Blundell, R., & MaCurdy, T. (1999). Labor Supply: A Review of Alternative Approaches.",
    "Eissa, N., & Hoynes, H. W. (2004). Taxes and the Labor Market Participation of Married Couples."
  ],
  "reasoning_summary": "While primary earners (mostly men) exhibit very low labor supply elasticities, secondary earners (typically married women) show significantly higher responsiveness. Empirical literature often places the compensated (substitution) elasticity for this group between 0.2 and 0.6, with substantial variance depending on whether the margin is participation (extensive) or hours worked (intensive). The estimate accounts for the reality that many microsimulation models calibrate toward the higher end to capture observed policy responses, while pure intensive-margin studies often yield more conservative figures."
}

runs.interpretation: The compensated (substitution) labor supply elasticity for secondary earners in a U.S. tax-benefit microsimulation model, representing the responsiveness of hours worked to changes in the net-of-tax wage rate.
citations: Blundell and MaCurdy (1999) Handbook of Labor Economics
Chetty et al. (2011) QJE
Heckman (1993) AER
runs.reasoningSummary: The literature consistently identifies secondary earners (often married women) as having significantly higher labor supply elasticities than primary earners. While earlier estimates were often larger, modern structural simulations and quasi-experimental studies suggest a modest positive elasticity. The range reflects uncertainty between structural models (which often yield higher elasticities) and reduced-form estimates (which are often smaller), with 0.3-0.4 being a widely used consensus range for calibration in microsimulation.

runs.interpretation: The compensated (substitution) labor supply elasticity for secondary earners in the U.S. labor market, typically used in tax microsimulation models to predict participation or hours worked responses to changes in marginal net-of-tax rates.
citations: Heckman, J. J. (1993). What has been learned about labor supply in the past twenty years?
Blundell, R., & MaCurdy, T. (1999). Labor supply: A review of alternative approaches.
Eissa, N., & Hoynes, H. W. (2004). Taxes and the labor market participation of married couples.
runs.reasoningSummary: Secondary earners (mostly married women) exhibit significantly higher labor supply elasticities than primary earners. While early literature often focused on hours-of-work elasticities (which are low), tax-benefit models often calibrate to the extensive margin (participation), which is more elastic. The chosen range reflects the consensus that while primary earner elasticities are near zero, secondary earners respond more robustly to tax incentives, typically in the 0.3-0.7 range for the participation-weighted substitution effect.
runs.rawResponse:
{
  "interpretation": "The compensated (substitution) labor supply elasticity for secondary earners in the U.S. labor market, typically used in tax microsimulation models to predict participation or hours worked responses to changes in marginal net-of-tax rates.",
  "point_estimate": 0.4,
  "quantiles": {
    "p05": 0.1,
    "p25": 0.25,
    "p50": 0.4,
    "p75": 0.65,
    "p95": 0.9
  },
  "citations": [
    "Heckman, J. J. (1993). What has been learned about labor supply in the past twenty years?",
    "Blundell, R., & MaCurdy, T. (1999). Labor supply: A review of alternative approaches.",
    "Eissa, N., & Hoynes, H. W. (2004). Taxes and the labor market participation of married couples."
  ],
  "reasoning_summary": "Secondary earners (mostly married women) exhibit significantly higher labor supply elasticities than primary earners. While early literature often focused on hours-of-work elasticities (which are low), tax-benefit models often calibrate to the extensive margin (participation), which is more elastic. The chosen range reflects the consensus that while primary earner elasticities are near zero, secondary earners respond more robustly to tax incentives, typically in the 0.3-0.7 range for the participation-weighted substitution effect."
}
